kit.referenceExcerpt: A feedback loop is a system in which the output of a process is fed back as input, modifying subsequent behaviour. Negative feedback stabilises systems by counteracting deviations from a target state; positive feedback amplifies deviations, leading to exponential change or instability.
kit.modelAnswer: A feedback loop is a mechanism where the output of a process affects its own input. Negative feedback stabilises a system by reducing deviations; positive feedback amplifies them. Examples are thermostats (negative) and microphone-speaker squeal (positive).

keyPoints.text: Definition: output feeds back into input

kit.learningObjective: Distinguish negative from positive feedback loops

studentAnswer: A feedback loop happens when the output of a system is routed back to its input. Negative feedback makes the system stable because it pushes back against changes, like a thermostat bringing the room back to 20 °C. Positive feedback amplifies changes instead — for example a microphone picking up its own speaker and producing a louder and louder howl.

keyPointsHit.evidence: output of a system is routed back to its input
Negative feedback makes the system stable

contentSignals.coherenceNote: Clear contrast with one concrete example for each type.

feedbackToStudent.whatWentWell: You clearly distinguish negative from positive feedback and illustrate both with apt examples.
feedbackToStudent.whatIsMissing: You could mention what happens to positive feedback without any damping — it often ends in saturation or breakdown.
feedbackToStudent.nextStep: Extend the microphone example: explain what limits the howl and why positive feedback rarely runs forever.

notes: All three key points addressed with evidence and examples. HIGH confidence.
